name: d1
context: Create databases, run SQL queries, and manage migrations on Cloudflare D1 — serverless SQLite at the edge
info: Cloudflare D1 is a serverless SQLite database that runs at the edge alongside Workers. D1 supports standard SQL syntax, automatic read replication, point-in-time recovery, and migrations via Wrangler. Databases are created per-account and bound to Workers via `wrangler.toml`. D1 uses SQLite semantics: ACID transactions, WAL mode, JSON functions, full-text search, and all standard SQL. Queries execute with low latency because the database is co-located with the Worker. Free tier: 500MB storage, 5 million rows read/day, 100K rows written/day. Common uses: user data, application state, metadata storage, content management, and analytics.
triggers: set up a database on cloudflare

sections.title: Creating databases and bindings
sections.body: Create a database with `wrangler d1 create <name>`. Add the `[[d1_databases]]` binding to `wrangler.toml` with `binding`, `database_name`, and `database_id`. The binding exposes a `D1Database` object on the Worker's `env`. Use `wrangler d1 list` to see all databases.

sections.title: Migrations
sections.body: Create migrations with `wrangler d1 migrations create <db> <name>`. Migrations are SQL files in the `migrations/` directory, numbered sequentially (0001_, 0002_). Apply with `wrangler d1 migrations apply <db> --remote` for production or `--local` for development. List status with `wrangler d1 migrations list <db> --remote`.

sections.title: Query API
sections.body: `env.DB.prepare(sql)` creates a prepared statement. `.bind(...values)` binds positional parameters. `.all()` returns `{ results, meta }`. `.first()` returns the first row. `.first('column')` returns a single column value. `.run()` executes without returning rows. `.raw()` returns rows as arrays. `.batch([stmts])` executes multiple statements in a single transaction.

sections.title: Batch transactions
sections.body: `env.DB.batch([stmt1, stmt2, ...])` executes multiple prepared statements in a single transaction. If any statement fails, all are rolled back. Use batch for multi-table inserts, data migrations, and any operation requiring atomicity. Each statement in the batch can be independently prepared and bound.

links.context: CLI for database and migration management
Use KV for simple key-value data instead of SQL

related.note: API token required for D1 operations
KV for key-value; D1 for relational data with SQL
Postgres for full-featured relational; D1 for edge-native SQLite
Supabase provides managed Postgres; D1 is serverless SQLite
